text: Literally, ‘whose eyes were shut up’, but the Hebrew text probably means ‘whose eyes were opened’.
Literally, according to the Latin, ‘tents the Lord has fixed’, but the word used in the Hebrew text seems to mean some kind of tree (perhaps the aloe), as indeed the context demands.

‘Shall rival Agag himself’; literally, ‘shall be lifted up close to Agag’. The Latin version can also be rendered, ‘shall be taken away because of Agag’, and the latter part of the verse, ‘his kingdom shall be taken away’ can be understood equally in reference to king Saul (I Kg. 15). But a prophecy on this subject is wholly unsuitable to the present context; and the Hebrew text has, ‘he shall be lifted up above Agag’. No Agag appears in scripture except the king defeated by Saul; but it may have been a title rather than a name (like ‘Pharao’), so that the point of the allusion remains uncertain.
According to the Hebrew text, ‘they shall swallow up the nations (that are) their enemies’.

‘The dealings there must be between this folk and thine’; in the Latin, ‘what thy folk shall do to this folk’, but according to the Hebrew text, ‘what this folk shall do to thy folk’. If the former is the true rendering, the allusion is perhaps to Balaam’s advice given on this occasion (31.16), not to the prophecy which here follows.

This can hardly be understood of the son of Adam, without destroying the parallelism of the verse; we should expect Moab to appear under another name. Some think the meaning is ‘sons of pride’ or ‘sons of tumult’ in the Hebrew.

The bearing, and in some places the meaning, of verses 20-24 is obscure. The word ‘nest’ in the Hebrew forms a play upon words with ‘Cinite’. In verse 24 ‘Italy’ seems to be a conjecture by the translator; the name used in the Hebrew text is Cethim, applied properly to Cyprus, but occasionally to the Mediterranean world in general. Nor is it certain that ‘the land of Heber’ refers to the Hebrews.
